topic: bespoke hardware sourcing for a penthouse

turns.content: I am deep in the details of a penthouse project and I have reached the stage that either elevates or ruins the entire design — hardware. Handles, hinges, cabinet pulls. The jewellery of architecture.
Hardware really is where the design lives at the fingertip level — it's what people touch every day. Are you looking at bespoke pieces or sourcing from established hardware houses?

Bespoke. There is no alternative for this project. The penthouse has a material palette built around patinated bronze and honed limestone. Every surface has been considered to the last millimetre. I am not going to punctuate that with catalogue hardware. The pulls need to be cast specifically for this interior, with a patina that matches the bronze cladding on the fireplace surround. But the fireplace was hand-patinated — it has variation, depth, the kind of surface that shifts under different light. If the hardware is too uniform, it will read as false against that wall.

So you need the hardware to have that same living quality — slight variation from piece to piece, a patina that looks developed rather than applied. That pushes you toward an artisan who can hand-finish each piece. Have you found anyone who can deliver at that level of craft while maintaining enough consistency across a full set?

There is a workshop I have been researching in Tuscany — a father-and-son operation near Pietrasanta. They cast bronze for sculptors and architects using traditional lost-wax, which gives a warmth to the surface that sand casting simply cannot achieve. Each piece retains the trace of the hand. I need thirty-two cabinet pulls, fourteen lever handles, six edge pulls, and two oversized statement pulls for the entry doors — those are the signature pieces, approximately four hundred millimetres long, sculptural in form.

Lost-wax casting for hardware is a serious commitment to craft, but for a penthouse where bronze is already a defining material, it makes complete sense. Fifty-four pieces total with the two entry pulls as hero elements. Are you designing those yourself, or collaborating with the workshop on the form?

I have sketched the profile — a tapered, asymmetric bar with a slight twist along its length, so the hand naturally falls into the grip. It references the spiral geometry in the penthouse ceiling detail. But I want the foundry to interpret it in bronze, not simply execute my drawing. The best artisan work happens in the conversation between the designer's intent and the material's behaviour. Bronze wants to do certain things. I want to let it.

That's a sophisticated approach — giving the artisan creative latitude within your design parameters so the material intelligence comes through. The twist detail tying back to the ceiling geometry is the kind of coherence that makes a project feel truly authored. When are you reaching out to them?

This week. I am sending my sketches and a full material specification. If their lead time works, this could be one of the most refined hardware installations I have ever delivered. Some details are simply not negotiable.

Your refusal to compromise on this is exactly what separates bespoke work from luxury-branded standard. The penthouse sounds extraordinary — the kind of project where someone picks up a door handle and realises that every single element was considered with that same level of intent.
